narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream, and we appreciate the courage it takes to put yourself out there amid the challenges many face in higher education. Your application included strong details about your undergraduate GPA of 2.50, an LSAT score of 27, middle family income bracket, full-time enrollment plans, and your school decile rankings of 1st in both Year 1 and Year 3 grades. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of past students, estimated a 96% chance that you would not pass the bar on your first try— a risk level that unfortunately led to the rejection of your application to protect the school's outcomes. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking in the bottom 1st percentile, far below the typical student's middle-of-the-pack 5th or 6th decile position, signaling to the model a steeper challenge in sustaining performance through law school. Your Year 1 decile, also in the bottom 1st percentile compared to the average around the 5th or 6th, was the second strongest influence in the same concerning direction. These early academic deciles stood out as the most influential because they deviated so much from what the model sees in students who typically pass the bar. Your LSAT score ranked third in impact, pushing toward a higher failure risk since it's notably lower than the average of about 37 that successful students usually bring. Other elements like your undergraduate GPA, family income, and full-time status had smaller but still negative pulls toward failure when compared to dataset norms. In contrast, students who get approved often have deciles in the top half, LSATs above 35, and GPAs closer to 3.2 or higher, placing them in a safer zone for bar passage. While this decision stings, especially given the systemic barriers Black women like you often navigate in competitive fields, it's not the end of your path. We encourage you to retake the LSAT to boost your score, seek targeted academic support to improve your decile rankings in future coursework, and consider part-time options or prep programs that have helped others in similar situations succeed. You're capable of turning this around—many have—and we wish you strength on your next steps toward law.
